title: Executive Order 11465—Inspection of Income, Excess-Profits, Estate, and Gift Tax Returns by the Committee on Internal Security, House of Representatives

flags.justification: This enables a congressional committee's investigative access rather than concentrating executive authority; it exercises the same standard tax-return congressional-inspection mechanism used routinely for other committees in this corpus (see EO 9430, EO 10907).
Access is bounded by specific, named Treasury regulations (Treasury Decisions 6132 and 6133).
A congressional information-access authorization, not a compliance regime.
No compliance regime is created by this order.
The authorization is explicitly bounded to 'during the Ninety-first Congress,' a defined, congressional-term-limited scope.
No existing oversight mechanism is removed.
No compliance regime is created by this order.

The recipient committee was the direct successor to the House Un-American Activities Committee (renamed in 1969), and the authorized scope (returns from 1950 to 1969) is notably broad, even though the underlying access mechanism itself is a standard, routinely-used congressional tool.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 1/12 = 8.3%. Historically notable: the Committee on Internal Security was the direct successor (renamed 1969) to the House Un-American Activities Committee. The underlying mechanism (Treasury-regulated congressional tax-return access) is standard and routinely used elsewhere in this corpus; the substantive notability here lies in the recipient committee's historical mandate and the breadth of the authorized scope, not in the architecture of the access mechanism itself.
